key_insight: Learning Boolean polynomial surrogates with uniform $L_\infty$ accuracy is fundamentally harder under noise, with optimal sample complexity scaling as $n^{d+1}$ for degree-$d$ polynomials and $ns^2$ for $s$-sparse Fourier-Walsh polynomials.
engineering_relevance: For optimization pipelines over binary decision spaces, the result tells you how many noisy samples are needed so surrogate-based optimization remains safe and reliable in the worst case.
method: Characterize minimax sample complexity for uniform estimation on the Boolean hypercube under subgaussian noise.
Prove tight lower bounds that apply even to adaptive learners.
Use auxiliary norms as proxies to control $L_\infty$ error when standard Fourier $L_2$ tools are insufficient.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports tight, matching sample-complexity rates from worst-case theory rather than empirical evaluation, and contrasts noisy uniform estimation with noiseless exact recovery.
watch_item: The guarantees are worst-case and noise-aware, so practical performance may depend strongly on problem structure and on whether the chosen surrogate class matches the true objective.
paper_title: Tight $L_\infty$ Sample Complexity for Low-Degree and Sparse Boolean Polynomials
